speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Brasg I'm conducting the interview with Bluma Doman. Today is the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa in English. Why did you want to leave Germany and settle in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who wanted-- who wanted to live in Germany? We had them enough for six years. We-- we all were very anxious to leave Germany, but took us four years because I wasn't well. My husband did a lot of nursing me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was actually wrong with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had pneumonia. My lung went out. It healed improper. So there was a doctor, a German doctor, [INAUDIBLE]. And he and my husband, daily nursed me back to-- to health.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you where you originally met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the-- we were all together friends in Ozorkow. That's why when I came home, we stayed five days. My husband said, let's go. We are getting married. And we clear out for that. When we came into Bamberg, we had to renew all our things. The German, [INAUDIBLE] with the civilian wedding. And we had some people from our city as witnesses. And we lived in Germany. It was very nice to live. But nobody wanted to stay there. Everybody immigrated. All America, or Israel, or maybe some other country. But everybody immigrated. That's why I'm asking my son if Germany has some Jews now. Poland has no Jews, only Warsaw, the capital. And they have in Krakow some Jews. In Ozorkow, there's not one Jew. One lived over there. When he died, they couldn't even bury him because Ozorkow cemetery-- I got a video. They make a forest from the cemetery. And the-- the stones were on the sidewalks. And-- and Lodz has only 150 Juden, which before the war Lodz had a quarter of a million [? Juden. ?] All the industrial, all the shops with furs belonged to Jews. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you keeping in touch with your brother and sister all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure And hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it impossible to get into Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother went into Israel as a child, straight to Israel. And my sister then went to Israel, was united with-- with my brother. They both lived together. And then my sister got married. And the son-in-law-- the brother-in-law adopted my son-- my brother. He's-- he's a little boy. He's going to stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did she marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: She married a man from Riga. He was also a concentration camp. He wasn't from [? Lita ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan [? Shapof ?]. He's a wonderful husband. So I had a wonderful husband for 35 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to get into Israel and join them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There-- there was a very hard life In 1945 in Israel. So in fact, after a few years when I became a citizen of-- of America, I brought down my sister. And then my sister brought down my brother. It-- it was at that time, it was a very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in-- in Los Angeles. My-- I was married to my husband for 35 years, had a wonderful life, made a nice living. I was always next to my husband. My husband would never go any place if I am not next to him. The same thing, he had to be next to me. He was very devoted. He was a very sweet man, just lived till 61 years. When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America. My daughter was born in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's American. This is Woonsocket. This is a part of-- of Woonsocket. There was-- the smallest state in the union is Providence, Rhode Island, yes? And this belonged to Providence, Rhode Island. The-- yes. We had-- we found-- when we arrived we found a uncle. My husband had an uncle before the war and three cousins. All those three girls were born in Ozorkow. And uncle left [INAUDIBLE] the army. He escaped to America. And then he brought down his wife and the three daughters, which we've been very close. Uncle died. He was 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Joint Distribution help you to get across to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Everything, they paid for everything. We came in a military plane because my son was just little, four and a half months old. So they said we cannot go by boat. The child will not withstand the journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited for a plane. I hesitate to go into a plane. My husband said, please, let's go. We've been, you know, in the German zones. We've been waiting our-- next to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us all in-- in a waiting camp. And little by little they brought us to America. America is a wonderful country. We lived in Los Angeles. We worked together. We had the cleaning store. I was-- my husband was tend to the cleaning. And I was the dress maker. We were making good. We raised the two children and not in poverty. But [INAUDIBLE] my son got married in 1973. He was-- he went to Israel for yeshiva. Then he came back. He went to New York. In New York, he got his [NON-ENGLISH]. Then he met Brenda. They got married. And they lived-- they came over here. Chana was born in America, in New York. And the rest of the children are all born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in Woonsocket, where we lived near our uncle. My husband did a wonderful job. We made a nice living at the beginning. And then my husband said, we go into business. So we went to Los Angeles. And we opened a business. We opened ourselves. It was very handy. You could do almost anything around the house. If you would see our house, everything, papering the walls, painting outside, inside, did you see my grandfather clock that I was staying in my room, this my husband made-- a hobby. And Sunday when we went into town from the shop, he put down his money, his hat. And he had a-- a radial saw, whatever you call it, a router, all the equipment. And he will stay in making it a lot of nice things in the house. All this happened when they decide he wants to make the dining room mirrors where the table sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he bought some wood, the old, old wood with the claws, you know, with the holes. He made one plank like this, the lumber, one plank of mirror. You should see it. You should see that wall. It was right after-- right at the table in our dining room. Was it easy to build a Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [? Why? ?] About near Los Angeles, there are a lot of, a lot of religious Juden. And my husband, if he couldn't go to shul, he was davening in-- in the store. When it was [NON-ENGLISH], he took out his yarmulke. He couldn't stay in the business with a yarmulke. He took off his yarmulke. He sent to the dressing room, which I had to fit, he went in. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Oy. My husband was a-- a religious minded Jew. That's all. His father was wearing also a [INAUDIBLE]. His mother wore a [INAUDIBLE]. But a religious Juden, that's all. And we stuck to it. I believe in it. I love it. I love to be here. It might sound a lot of rules is bending a little bit for me. Like, I took sick on [INAUDIBLE]. The doctor came and said, she has to be rushed to the hospital. She has a blood clot in her left foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to the doctor, can we wait till the evening for the-- for the second day of [NON-ENGLISH]? Can we wait till the evening that I take my mother to the hospital. The doctor said no. So he said, you know what, doctor? We can walk down the [? Linsfield ?] clinic. And they'll x-ray my mother's foot. And they'll tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we came into [? Lindsfield ?], Brenda with him, never by himself. They're also like us. They're always together. If she goes, he goes. If he goes, she goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And went in the hospital. They made the x-rays. You know, and they found not only one clot, but a lot of them. The doctor calls in my son. And he tells him this is the situation. So my son said to him, can we wait till the evening? He said, if it would be my mother, I wouldn't do it. I would go to the hospital right now. So what? Brenda went to the phone, which they never do. She called an ambulance. And we rode Brenda and Mosche, we rode down to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son said, if you don't want to go, it's Yontif. She said, no. You're going. I'm going. We stayed over there till the Yontif was ending. I was ready to go to Israel, you see, the second day of [NON-ENGLISH]. And so I wind up in the hospital. And my son, a maid too-- the maid was with us. We have two maids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was with us. And we stayed to the evening. Then they called the sister. Brenda has a sister here. And she came and picked them up. They went home. When-- I see that you're in a wheelchair. When did you first discover your illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1970. I took sick. I went-- my son went, 1970. He had-- he's a brilliant [INAUDIBLE]. At 20 years he had already elementary school, high school, and he had three years of UCLA. You know UCLA in Los Angeles. It's very hard to get there. Three years. And he was 20 years old. He graduated with cum laude. And then he said he wants to go to Israel. So my husband said, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Israel, was in Israel. As we went to the airport-- and I was heartbroken. My daughter and her boyfriend of hers, my husband-- and there was a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] over there for-- because he joined the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were making dancing at the airport. And as we came home, I opened the door from the car. And I just fell out. My feet said, goodbye. That much from the sidewalk-- I still have scars. I fell by the sidewalk. If I would fell on the sidewalk, I would be a goner. My husband took me in with my daughter's boyfriend. And the next day I went to x-ray my foot. It wasn't broken. But it was strained. Had a-- the muscles. And since then my troubles started. We'd go from doctor to doctor. Nobody knew what I have. I had been hospitalized so many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what myelograms is? I had three myelograms. It's awful test. I had everything done. They couldn't for 10 years-- they couldn't detect what's wrong with me. So after 10 years, I start working with my husband at the store. For seven years, I walked every day with them holding on to him with a cane. I came into the shop. I sat by the machine. I was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my son called us. He's engaged. He is going to be married. So we get a call to the business if we can go to Johannesburg. My husband said, I don't even know where Johannesburg is. I cannot schlep money that far to New York. So he said, OK. The wedding is going to be in New York. We went to the wedding. I walked already with a cane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody, you know, was-- people from our city said, how can you do that with this face, you walking with a cane? My husband said, do you have any ideas what I should do? He was, you know, heartbroken when people asked him. Because we tried everything. And my husband said, you have any idea? Come. Give me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- a friend of ours, was my husband's, a good friend, he also died a few years ago. He said, I having trouble with my feet too and my spine. Come. I'm going for acupuncture here in New York. He came from Atlanta, Georgia, for the wedding of my son's. He said come. Next day I'm going. I can take Bluma with me. So he said, no. If there's acupuncture, I go home, and I'll find it. It's illegal in America. They sitting. You should see it, patients on the floor for acupuncture. Finally, my daughter got a-- a appointment. They took me. My husband went with me in. And as I turned around, my husband said, don't move. You will sit next to me. You have a lot, a lot of needles in your spine. I was going for one year once a week for acupuncture. I came back. I was aching. I couldn't work better. All of a sudden I said to my husband, it's enough. So he said, I didn't want to ask you because you would think I don't want to spend the money. But it didn't do you any good, just make you pain. You come home. You have to go to bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we quit. Then every-- everybody said something, I even had hypnosis. Nothing. My feet didn't-- after 10 years, the doctors gave me a test of my eyes and my ears. They said I have multiple sclerosis. So my husband said to the doctor, what can we do? He said, she can live to 100. But she'll never walk. My husband wanted to kill that doctor. He said, for 10 years you're pushing me around. Now you're telling me she won't? So we went out. We bought a wheelchair. My husband brought home the wheelchair. I said, I'm not going in this chair because I'm not going out that somebody should see me in the wheelchair. Me? I said, what? I was a devil at home, singing, dancing, bicycle riding, ice skating. Now, they're going to see me in a chair. He said, so what do you want me to do? I'm helpless. We had quit the business because I couldn't go anymore to work, and he was scared to leave me alone in the house. And with-- I walked with crutches. I cleaned my house. When he walked out, he said, oh, I-- I know what you're going to do now. I had already in mind. On my knees, just like my mother, I stayed and polished my furniture. And he came back. He said, you don't have to tell me. I see you did. But I couldn't. You know? So finally, my-- all of the sudden one morning in November, first my husband went to the doctor. He was with me. The appointment was for me. My husband was taking me to the doctor. As we're sitting in the office, my husband said, you know what? I'm not feeling well. I'm going in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw Dr. [INAUDIBLE] already walking by. I'll go in. He knocked on the door. He said, come in, Charles. He took his blood pressure. He said, Charles, you're going this minute to the hospital. It was a new hospital, not the [INAUDIBLE]. And from that hospital was a bridge. And there were doctors' offices.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, you're going straight to the hospital. He said, no, doctor, I'll go home. I'll arrange for my wife who should take care of it. Then I'll check in tomorrow morning. He said, this is not possible. You not going into the car. You're going straight to the hospital So he went to the hospital. This was Monday morning. On Sunday afternoon, my doctor said-- he came in. He said to me, Charles suffered a deadly stroke. He is a piece of vegetable.

speaker: interviewee
text: You better send for your children. My daughter was just six months married. She lived in Canada. We went to her wedding. Like-- thank god that he was with me at the wedding. And he said, send for your children. So I called my daughter. My daughter came right away with her husband. And then they called Mosche, and Mosche came home. And eight days later, next Monday, was the funeral. Mosche came home Sunday night because he left [INAUDIBLE]. He came home. And he called me from New York, that he's in New York. I said daddy had just died. So he came home. As he came home, he said to me, Mommy, you're going home with me. I'm not leaving you here by yourself. I said, Rebecca is in Canada. And you are here. My daughter is married to a wonderful fellow in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now she lives in America. She has only one son. Her son is 14 years-- last year he was bar mitzvah. That shows she's doing very well. Nice. And so is my son. But I had to come over here because my son said, Daddy is gone. Who's going to take care of you like Daddy did? So I came over here. And-- I stayed 15 years. 15 years was November last, so long I-- I-- At least I had a wonderful life. We've been together. Everything we did was together with a wonderful man. That's it. He was 61 years old. So teach, God, what to do. And I was a sick one. I was in the wheelchair. And he died. This was-- it was such tragedy in Los Angeles. Everybody who came to the funeral and said, what are you going to do now? We know how Charles took care of you. So my daughter said, she's going home with my brother. This was the best bet because over here, I had a maid. In America, you can't afford this. So I came here. There was-- my son stayed with me one month. We sold everything we make. And very quick we losing everything. The house we left in-- in attorney's-- power of attorney. A year later, they sold the house. So now I'm here. Tell me, what is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca. Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David. And the little boy is named after my husband, which is Charles, Aaron Charles Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your daughter in Canada. Or is she back in America now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She lives in America now. She lived in Canada just about a year after they've been married. They live in Florida, in Hollywood. They have-- my son-in-law is a stock broker, very good looking, tall guy. And my daughter is a really beautiful. He said to me after the wedding. He said, Mom, everything I wanted in a wife is Rebecca, if she was just a little bit taller. She's-- she's not really little. But he's tall. That's-- the only son. She has wonderful in-laws. And Mommy stays here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Rebecca do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What do you do? Spend the-- spend the husband's money. What does she have to do? Nothing. They have only one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren here? Tell me about your grandchildren that you live with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they're my life. The grandchildren. Chana was four months old in New York. My [INAUDIBLE] Brenda and-- and Chana, when she was four months old to Los Angeles. They stayed with us two or three months. Then they went to South Africa. This was a ball. When we were bathing her, you should see it. We were sitting around watching how Brenda's bathing her. It was a thrill. It's just too bad that my husband didn't see all his grandchildren. I came over here. I-- yes. When I took so sick, my husband said, after Mosche's marriage, he said I want you to go to-- to Johannesburg and see. I'm sure if you go see Mosche, that she's all right, you come back. You'll be cured.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mosche came home to America, had just bought this house, a month or six weeks had bought this house, not this one, the smaller house. Mosche came home. He took me to-- to Los-- over here to Johannesburg for one month.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband said, when Mom's coming back now, she's gonna be walking. She's-- I will see her a month. Yedidya was eight months old. Chana was five or six. And Beila. This what hurts me. My husband didn't live to see none of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I-- I came that time a month. I came back. My husband said, no news? I said, you want me to walk? The doctor said a miracle couldn't even help me. It affects-- my sickness affects all my muscles and all my nervous system. So I came back after Mosche [INAUDIBLE] house. And then there was teaching. Mosche was working on a job. I came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was happy. I saw the three grandchildren. And then when-- when Chana-- no when Yedidya was eight months old, I came over here. And I went back home. I didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Mosche came another time. And he said, Daddy, I want you to come and Mommy together. No. So when I-- when he died, Brenda was pregnant with Rochel, see? When I came over here. So the three children, the little ones are grown up with me. See?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was keeping them. I was more-- more active than I'm now. But the children were my life. I was singing to them. I was-- in fact, when Rochel, was sick-- sometimes I lived in the house. I had one room. And when Rochel was sick, I was quicker to go into his room and schlep her out from the-- from the crib. And I held her on me. And Brenda comes in. I'm sitting. She was [? hot ?] when I'm sitting holding the baby.
